title: Photoelectric effect
short: Light ejects electrons from a metal only when each photon carries enough energy; the leftover energy is the electron's kinetic energy.
full:
When light strikes a metal surface it can knock out electrons — but only if the photon energy hf exceeds the metal's work function W, the minimum energy needed to free an electron. Brighter light below that threshold ejects nothing; the threshold depends on frequency, not intensity.

Einstein explained this by treating light as photons of energy E = hf. Energy conservation gives the maximum kinetic energy of the ejected electron: KE_max = hf − W. This particle picture of light earned Einstein the Nobel Prize and helped launch quantum theory.
